figures.caption: Fig. 3: Graphical explanation of Alg. 3. On the left, the the nominal distribution P̂t and the atoms {x̂(1), . . . , x̂(5)} in its support. On the right, the worst-case distribution P ∗, supported on {x̂(1), x̂(2∗), x̂(3∗), x̂(4), x̂(4∗), x̂(5)}, and which minimizes the probability of xt belonging to Xobs. In this example x̂(1) ∈ Xobs, so it is not perturbed, and εt is such that it allows to fully transport x̂(2) and x̂(3) to the boundary of Xobs, but only the fraction m0 of mass from x̂(4), and no mass from x̂(5). Note that the set Xobs has arbitrary shape.

Fig. 7: Plot of search trees after 60 seconds. The number of nodes are 361240, 579358, and 30063, for Momentbased, Confidence, and Bandit Checkers, respectively. The Bandit Checker finds a solution with nominal trajectory in red.

TABLE I: Benchmark results for 4-D linear system over 100 runs. ‘Succ’ is the fraction of trials in which a valid path was found within 300 s; ‘Time’ is the mean planning time over successful trials; ‘–’ indicates no successful trial. Best success rate in each row is bold; among methods with equal success, the fastest is also bold.

Fig. 6: Benchmark environments for 4-D Linear System. Obstacles are shown in black and the goal region is shown in green. Representative plots of Monte carlo simulation of solution trajectories are shown in blue for Gaussian noise.
